title: Context-dependent reconfiguration and spatial reorganisation of the miR-199a-5p/XIAP axis in high-grade serous ovarian cancer
abstract: Background: High-grade serous ovarian cancer (HGSOC) remains a leading cause of gynaecological cancer mortality, largely owing to late diagnosis and the emergence of platinum resistance. A defining feature of resistant disease is impaired apoptotic execution despite persistent survival signalling. XIAP is a key inhibitor of caspase activity, and microRNAs are important modulators of stress-response pathways; however, the functional relationship between individual miRNAs and their targets may vary across cellular states. Methods: We investigated the miR-199a-5p/XIAP axis by integrating (i) cross-species structural and CLIP-informed support for miR-199a-5p binding to the XIAP 3'UTR, (ii) functional perturbation in paired cisplatin-sensitive (A2780) and cisplatin-resistant (A2780cis) ovarian cancer cell models, and (iii) spatial characterisation in human ovarian tissue using multiplex fluorescence in situ hybridisation and immunofluorescence. Quantitative image analysis was used to assess signal distribution and co-localisation at single-cell resolution. Results: miR-199a-5p did not show a uniform repressive relationship with XIAP across cellular contexts. In cisplatin-sensitive cells, miR-199a-5p was consistent with basal XIAP repression and was associated with enhanced apoptotic responses following cisplatin exposure. In resistant cells, this relationship was attenuated, and cisplatin treatment was accompanied by divergence between XIAP levels and apoptotic readouts. Spatial analysis of human FFPE specimens showed tumour-associated reorganisation of this axis. Non-neoplastic ovary displayed compartmental segregation, with XIAP enriched in epithelial structures and miR-199a-5p relatively restricted to stromal regions. In HGSOC, this organisation was altered, with broader miR-199a-5p distribution, greater spatial overlap with XIAP, and a distinct perinuclear, occasionally nuclear-associated, miR-199a-5p pattern not observed in control tissue. Conclusions: These findings indicate that the functional relationship between miR-199a-5p and XIAP is context-dependent and altered in cisplatin-resistant states. The identification of a tumour-associated spatial phenotype in human HGSOC underscores the value of spatially resolved approaches for understanding regulatory heterogeneity and supports their potential utility in biomarker development and in the identification of therapeutic vulnerabilities linked to platinum-resistant disease.
